speaker: interviewee
text: --train to Germany, to Hamburg, a suburb of Hamburg, called Ochenzoll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Ochenzoll?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ochen-- O-C-H-E-N-- zoll-- Z-O, double L-- Ochenzoll. That was the suburb's name. And there was a camp for 200 young girls. And there-- I said that before, but I don't know I should repeat it-- with one doctor and a nurse. And there I came as the second nurse. And there, the conditions were, compared to Auschwitz, human. I mean, I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, the camp had a little-- it was called the hospital. It was two little rooms-- one for us and one for when the patients came. Before they went to work in the morning, they came in if they had any problems-- any injuries, or something. And there was a-- a hospital room with about four beds. But never-- we had no patients ever. Because everybody knew that to be sick-- to be sick, it's very dangerous. Because they will send you straight to Auschwitz when you are sick. So nobody was ever sick. But they came in with headaches and little injuries, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a factory nearby-- some sort of factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the girls worked in the ammunition factory. They-- they worked in two shifts. It was the day shift and the night shift. And before the girls went to work or came back to work, they always called in if they needed something-- medicine, or some care, or advice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived-- and you lived there as well as worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't work. I didn't work in the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. I beg your pardon. I meant, you were the nurse there, but did you live there at the-- as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. That-- that-- there was a little room. And there was the doctor and the two nurses. And we-- we brought the the-- from the kitchen. From the lager kitchen, we brought the warm water. There was possibility to wash, to keep clean, to-- I mean, nearly human conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, we-- there was more edible food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to leave the area at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was with electric. It-- it was a concentration camp-- only a small one.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say small, what size? How many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 200-- 200 girls were sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left each day to go to work, and then came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- 100 during the day, 100 at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I arrived there, I say, in October.

speaker: interviewee
text: End-- end of October, '44. And I stayed there till March '44. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you mean 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: 5, March. Then from there, we marched. Then the death march started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- they completely evacuated the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They completely evacuated the camp. And everybody, we just walked. We didn't know why, what. We just walked, and walked, and walked for, I think about three weeks. Because we didn't have watches. We didn't have calendars. We-- I don't know exactly. But about three weeks we just walked and stayed overnight in farms, slept in hay, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no idea where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the 200 healthy girls who started-- and they were falling like flies. Every-- everyday somebody was dropping dead. It's-- it was horrible. Because we had no food, no-- no-- no room, I mean, very bad. Until finally, we arrived in Bergen-Belsen, which was another horror camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you finally got there after the three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few, very few. But that was so much to the end that there was absolute disorganization there in Bergen-Belsen. And there were mountains of dead bodies lying around there. There was no one anymore to carry them away, or burn them, or whatever-- no food, no nothing. And luckily, in three days time, the English arrived. And they liberated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were only there for three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only three days in that Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people alive in Bergen-Belsen as well-- when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ghosts-- ghosts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know-- many.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You didn't see anybody you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know anyone. Everybody I knew, I left in Auschwitz when I left to the-- to that infectious barrack. Since then on, I had no friend, no relative, nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't see any of them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any one of them. One cousin returned-- one cousin. She's younger than me. She's three years younger. She still lives in Levice. She was already here to visit me twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were liberated from Bergen-Belsen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --which unit was it that did the liberating?

speaker: interviewee
text: France ambulance unit-- English-- they were young fellows-- very nice. They arrived in tanks and all that. We were happy. We were happy to see them. And they said, go looting. And we-- we were-- whoever could walk [LAUGHS] went to the village. And on the way, there was a garden. And I saw rhubarb. And I took the whole bunch out of the ground. And I started eating that rhubarb. And it was very sour. But it was beautiful. And as I was eating, and eating, and eating-- and my mouth got completely burned, that-- it was-- it was all raw and sore. So when I returned to the camp, and they were already waiting us there with good, rich food, I couldn't eat at all. And that was another reason that I stayed alive. Because I-- I was always very greedy on food. And if I would have-- wouldn't have that sore in my mouth, I would have eaten and eaten. And many, many young, healthy girls died there, because of that big contrast between the starvation and the rich food. Then later on, they learned that we are not allowed to have so quickly normal food. And we were shortly disinfected, and cleaned, and sent over next to Bergen-Belsen, and was the clean Bergen-Belsen, where the officers live, the German officers-- the SS. But of course, they all fled. And it was all empty. And we, the few survivors, we went over there. It was nice and clean, like for officers. And there we were waiting for the transportation home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did the British stay? Did they stay in the camp, or did they just come, and liberate you, and go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think they organized then that other Bergen-Belsen. And all-- yes, it was a-- under their control, everything, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told people where to go and what to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There everybody got displaced there and waited. And then the waiting became so very, very, very long and boring.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was it? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that I was in there about? Weeks, weeks. Oh, and that was a beautiful episode after three or four weeks. One beautiful summer day, they said that we will have a concert. Yehudi Menuhin will play for us. And it was a beautiful summer afternoon-- green grass. We were sitting there on the grass. And he was-- he was on a small stage there in shorts, short sleeves. He was young. He was there about 25, 26. And he was playing on the violin. And I was just sitting there. And that's the first time I realized what happened to me, that here I am on my own, not a friend, not a relative. And my tears started to roll-- the first time in all that time. Yehudi Menuhin-- he came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, how-- how long-- how-- did the-- did they keep having people come and look after you, or did they eventually leave the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, they left the camp. We started their sewing from blankets. We made suits. And I mean, we started to-- to look a little bit normal by that time. Our hair was grown, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there in that officer's section? How many of you were left in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, many, many-- hundreds. It was big. It was big-- all different nationalities from everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: All over Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: From all over Europe, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there for some weeks. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then-- then it was too boring to do nothing. So then I said-- and there was a small hospital there for-- for the sick people. Because were-- there, where I lived, in the officer's quarters, were only the healthy ones. And who was sick was in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said that I'm a nurse. And I would like to work in the hospital. So I worked there in the hospital. Was there any equipment or any drugs available?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everything. That was already run by the-- by the-- by the English. That was a proper hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- to help people get well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to help people, yes, to restore them back to health, and send them home. That was a normal hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay nursing in that hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for-- I-- I met there my-- I met there a doctor. We fell in love. And we married there. And I became pregnant. And we returned to Prague, in April, the 6th of April. And the 7 of April, my daughter was born. Of course, from all the traveling, and all the excitement, she was born on seven months. She was very premature. And-- but the next day, when we arrived home, she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in Prague? Did you find somewhere to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband had there some people he knew. And we had a room there. So-- and I went straight to the hospital. And when I came out from the hospital, my-- I had a daughter. Her name is Vera. And they didn't let her home with me, because she was very delicate, very small. So she was kept there in the hospital. I didn't have any milk. So they arranged for a special young mother, who was feeding her. And I was just visiting every day to see her. And in the meantime, we organized. We had a place. And then after two months, she came home. And-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a healthy young girl. But when she was born, she was a kilo 20. Because both parents came from the concentration camp, that she got special care.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband was in a concentration camp too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well he-- yes. And he worked there in the-- in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how long were you in Prague then with your daughter and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long were we in Prague?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was '46 April, when we arrived, until '49. But in the meantime, I divorced. And in '49, we went to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in Prague yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or did you just keep looking after your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just looking after my child. I also went back to uni. But that was more or less-- I-- I didn't do much with it. And then in Vienna, we waited 11 months to come, to get the permit to come to Australia-- not the permit, the visa-- to come to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, when you were staying in Vienna, were you working there, or were you on your own? Was your husband with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on-- I-- [LAUGHS] I was between two husbands. [LAUGHS] The-- the one was the divorced. And with the other one, I couldn't marry, because I wasn't divorced yet. But in Vienna, everything was settled. I divorced the one, and I married the other one. And I came with the second one out to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, your second husband, he was not a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My second husband died 15 months ago today. He was an engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of engineering work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mechanical-- mechanical.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he worked as an engineer in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Vienna as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. In Vienna, we couldn't-- we didn't work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a displaced persons camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had a rented room, because luckily, my parents had a-- a big-- big house in Vienna. How do you call it? Department-- not department.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart--

speaker: interviewee
text: Apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: An apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Apartment house in Vienna. And during all those war years, the rent was coming in. And so we had some money in Vienna there to live from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, so were you able to live in the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was already rented to someone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was rented to people. But the rent, which they payed to the agent, was there. And the agent gave it to us. So we were able to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pay the passage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Paid for our room and for the food. And-- [LAUGHS] and waited all the time for-- for the visa to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: It took 11 months for the visa to turn up?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took 11 months because of the complication that my husband already had a permit. But he was then single. So in the meantime, he married me. So we had to put me on his permit. The permit came back to Australia. While they put me on his permit, it came back. Then it finally came back. Then the visa, it was a problem with the visa. But then the Korean War broke out. And everybody got the visa. So we came. So we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, so how did you come to Australia? By what means?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Genoa-- from Genoa we boarded the Sorriento. And we arrived here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Sorrento full of people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah!

speaker: interviewer
text: --who were-- who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Full? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --who were migrating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Only it was only us. And it was about-- about-- it was about 2,000 more people on it than they should be. Oh, we had a fantastic time-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: For--

speaker: interviewee
text: --very crammed but very, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all from places like Hungary, or were they from all over Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Italians as well and all different people as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I was mostly with Hungarian and Czechoslovakian people together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they mostly Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- have you any idea how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Fremantle, how long did it take for you to get to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Fremantle, we stayed only one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, but how long was the-- was the boat trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a month. It-- it was a very rough trip. But it took us a month. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not saying. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We were happy. What do you do on a ship? Only it wasn't so comfortable as when you go on a cruise. But we were together. And we-- we had good food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the accommodation was the girls were separate. And the men were separate. Now, it wasn't so-- on that big immigration boat, you don't have such a big luxuries. But it was all right. It was all right. Then we arrived to Fremantle. First we went to Colombo. Then there we had a nice day; then to Fremantle. From there we went into Purse for one day. Then we went to Melbourne, also for a day or two. And then we arrived to Sydney. And here our best friends were waiting for us. My husband studied with him. And through them, I got to know my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Sydney, whereabouts did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived on Bondi Beach. They rented a room for us on Bondi Beach. I forgot the name of the hotel. It doesn't exist anymore. But that was so terribly expensive. We couldn't-- we couldn't afford that. So we-- we went around. And we found a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was your husband allowed to work as an engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was straight away-- engineers were in such a big demand. But anyhow, he didn't work. He couldn't speak English. Neither could I. He didn't work as an engineer. He worked as a draftsman.

speaker: interviewer
text: For--

speaker: interviewee
text: He got a job straight away. And I-- [LAUGHS] I got a job as a machinist.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- for a clothing company?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a clothing company-- shirts-- men's shirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few unsuccessful attempts, I finally learned how to-- how to work on the machine and how to-- and I was a machinist for nearly seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the company that you worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many companies, [LAUGHS] I-- because they-- they were sending me away. I wasn't very good. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- so you-- you sewed [LAUGHS] very many shirts in very many companies.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sew-- not shirts, only part of shirts. On one place, I only did the cuffs. On the other place, I only did that. But gradually, l learned the trade. And then I-- and then I ended up in a factory where I did skirts-- whole skirts. And I was so good in it that I worked in piece work. And I made five skirts in one hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: The boss said I am the quickest skirt machinist in Sydney. Well, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I worked there for a long time. It doesn't exist anymore. It became Suit Master. But when-- when I worked there, it had a different name. It doesn't exist anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from-- till what year did you work there? I mean, what year did you finish being a machinist?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in '50. Until '57 I worked as a machinist. Well, in between I had three miscarriages, and so a little bit off work, on work. And-- and then I thought, that's enough. And I started. I looked for office work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any study?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job. And I studied at night. And I-- I became a bookkeeper. And I stayed there for 21 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you work for for all that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: John J. Hilton, frock-- frock manufacturers. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. When you were-- came to Australia, you said you spoke no English at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not all is not quite correct. I-- I learned English. And I read English. But nobody understood me when we arrived, really. And it-- I can say it was no English at all. It took about 1/2 a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to classes, or did you just practice?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to classes, yes. In-- they were special evening classes for-- for migrants. We went to classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your husband both learned English together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned English together. And then as soon as he could speak, he became an-- he-- he changed his job, and he-- he was a proper engineer. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have someone to look after your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- I-- we always managed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, you didn't have any more children after Vera.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just had three unsuccessful attempts.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And tell me, when you finished working for John J. Hilton, did you do any other work after that, or did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then I did some part-time one day, two day, for different firms and-- until-- until recently, say three, four years ago, when I stopped working completely. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, what-- do you have any particular hobbies or anything that you do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hobbies?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm? Any particular pastimes that you do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I go to different lectures, frame of the [NON-ENGLISH] University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are you studying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyday different. For example, today I had Philosophy. It was very interesting-- about Chinese philosophy; on Tuesday, Music; on Wednesday, Psychology; on Thursday, Philosophy one week; the next one, Current Affairs; and on Friday, Theatre Appreciation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you go each week?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go every day. And I fill in my time, because since I lost my husband, I have to fill in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you play cards or do anything else like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to play bridge. But-- but when my daughter married, I-- I had too much time. I-- I played bridge. But then when my first grandchild was born, I left it. And I never-- I-- I didn't play ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: A girl, Karen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a boy, Robby-- Robert-- 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they both live in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Sydney. They live in Dover Heights, not far from here, five minutes by car. And they are fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, when you look back at what happened to you, you said to me originally that there were two miracles-- one that you had done the nursing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The miracles, well, everybody who can tell this story, who survived, has some miracles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a special reason that you think you were saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: I call it miracles. They-- they were accidents or incidents. You didn't know if you are doing the right thing or the wrong thing. It-- things were just happening to you. And to me, it was for my good what had happened. Because when I couldn't study medicine and I study nursing, that was my lifesaver. Because I would have never stepped out where they said, 'Qualified nurses step out.' If I wouldn't have been a nurse-- so that has its reason that I made that course. Then when-- that saved, actually, my life. Because that's how-- how I-- I wouldn't have survived under the conditions working in a factory or so. I was a nurse. And I had better conditions-- human conditions. And I survived it. Then when we were freed, liberated, I-- I was such a big eater and so eager on food. And if I wouldn't have that sore on my mouth, I would have eaten myself to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to stop for a moment while we change the tape.
